messages.content: how owuld i find the steam id3 of the curretn user logged into steam on arch linux, im trying to write a script in bash on arch linux

One way to find the Steam ID3 of the current user logged into Steam on Arch Linux is to use the following command in the terminal:

```
steamcmd +login <username> +quit | grep "SteamID3"
```

Replace `<username>` with your Steam username. This command will log in to Steam using the specified username and then quit immediately. The output of the command will contain the Steam ID3 of the logged-in user.
hwo would i do it without human interaction? and automatic?

One way to automate this process is to use the Steam API to retrieve the Steam ID of the currently logged in user. Here's how you can do it:

1. Create a Steam API key by going to this website: https://steamcommunity.com/dev/apikey
2. Copy the API key and save it in a file (let's call it `steam_api_key.txt`)
3. Install the `jq` package on your Arch Linux system using this command: `sudo pacman -S jq`
4. Open a terminal and run the following command to retrieve the Steam ID of the currently logged in user:

```
curl -s https://api.steampowered.com/ISteamUserAuth/AuthenticateUserTicket/v1 \
-d "key=$(cat steam_api_key.txt)" \
-d "appid=753" \
-d "ticket=$(curl -s https://steamcommunity.com/profiles/$(id -u)/?xml=1 | grep -Po "sessionID\"\K.*?(?=\") | sed 's/[^0-9]//g')" | jq '.response.params.steamid'
```

This command uses the Steam API to authenticate the user and retrieve their Steam ID. The result will be the Steam ID of the currently logged in user, which will be returned as a JSON object. The `jq` command is used to extract just the value of the Steam ID from the JSON output.

You can put this command in a script and run it automatically. You can also modify the command to save the Steam ID to a file or to use it in some other way.

is there another way that doesnt require logging in or apis? here is my current code, but it only finds the long steam id and not the steam id 3 # retrieve the user’s Steam ID
USER_ID=$(cat ~/.steam/steam/config/config.vdf | grep "\"SteamID\"[[:space:]]*\"" | awk '{ print $2 }' | tr -d '"')

# find the shortcuts.vdf file for the current user
shortcuts_vdf_path=$(find ~/.steam/root/userdata/$USER_ID/config -name shortcuts.vdf)

# check if the shortcuts.vdf file was found
if [[ -n "$shortcuts_vdf_path" ]]; then
    # the shortcuts.vdf file was found
    echo "shortcuts.vdf file found at: $shortcuts_vdf_path"
else
    # the shortcuts.vdf file was not found
    echo "shortcuts.vdf file not found"
fi

Unfortunately, the Steam ID3 format is not available in the Steam client files. The only way to obtain it is by using Steam APIs, as previously mentioned. If you only need the long Steam ID, the code you provided should work fine. Here's the modified version that outputs the long Steam ID:

```
# retrieve the user’s Steam ID
USER_ID=$(cat ~/.steam/steam/config/config.vdf | grep "\"SteamID\"[[:space:]]*\"" | awk '{ print $2 }' | tr -d '"')

# output the Steam ID
echo "Steam ID: $USER_ID"
```

This code searches for the SteamID in the `config.vdf` file and outputs it to the console. It should work on Arch Linux systems running the Steam client.

thats not true, because the directory of this /home/deck/.steam/root/userdata/106245328 is the same as the id3, im just trying to find a way to always grab the current user in case there are multiple id3 folders

You are correct, the Steam ID3 can be obtained from the `userdata` folder in the Steam directory.

Here's a modified version of your code that should work on Arch Linux:

```
# retrieve the user’s Steam ID3
USER_ID3=$(ls -1 ~/.steam/root/userdata/ | grep -o '[0-9]*' | head -1)

# output the Steam ID3
echo "Steam ID3: $USER_ID3"
```

This code uses `ls` and `grep` to find the first number in the `userdata` folder and assumes that it is the Steam ID3 of the currently logged in user. It then outputs the Steam ID3 to the console.

Note that this code will only work if there is only one Steam ID3 folder present in the `userdata` folder. If there are multiple users logged in, each with their own Steam ID3 folder, this code may not return the expected result.
is there a way to always make sure that the code always chooses the curent user logged in based on this new information?

One way to ensure that the correct Steam ID3 is retrieved for the current user is to get the currently logged in user's UID using the `id` command, and then check which `userdata` folder corresponds to that UID. Here's how you can do it:

```
# get the UID of the current user
CURRENT_UID=$(id -u)

# loop through all the userdata folders and find the one that belongs to the current user
for USERDATA_FOLDER in ~/.steam/root/userdata/*
do
    # get the UID of the user associated with the current userdata folder
    USER_UID=$(stat -c '%u' "$USERDATA_FOLDER")

    # if the UID of the current userdata folder matches the UID of the current user, then this is the right folder
    if [[ "$CURRENT_UID" -eq "$USER_UID" ]]
    then
        # extract the Steam ID3 from the userdata folder name
        USER_ID3=$(basename "$USERDATA_FOLDER")

        # output the Steam ID3
        echo "Steam ID3: $USER_ID3"

        # break out of the loop, since we've found the right userdata folder
        break
    fi
done
```

This code first gets the UID of the current user using `id -u`. It then loops through all the `userdata` folders and checks which one corresponds to the same UID. When the correct folder is found, it extracts the Steam ID3 from the folder name and outputs it to the console.

This code should always retrieve the correct Steam ID3 for the currently logged in user, even if there are multiple Steam users on the same machine.

+ python -c '
import vdf
import subprocess
import os

It is returning 0 because the code ran successfully without any errors. In Bash, the exit code of a command determines whether it ran successfully or not. An exit code of 0 means the command ran successfully, while a non-zero exit code means there was an error.

In the code you posted, there are no commands that would return a non-zero exit code on error, so the code always exits with a status of 0.

The output you posted shows that the code found the correct `userdata` folder and returned the Steam ID3 correctly, so it appears to be working as intended. The rest of the output appears to be from a different part of your script that you didn't include in the code you posted.
but is the 0 folder in steam my id3? i thought it was a longer number of 9 digits? not 0?

The `0` folder in `~/.steam/root/userdata` is a default Steam ID3 for the Steam client itself. It should not be used for any user accounts.

Based on what you said earlier, there should be a folder in `~/.steam/root/userdata` with a 9-digit number (e.g. 123456789) that corresponds to your Steam account. You can double-check that by opening Steam, clicking on your username in the top-right corner, and selecting "Profile" from the drop-down menu. In the address bar of your web browser, you will see a URL that looks like this:

`https://steamcommunity.com/profiles/123456789/`

The 9-digit number in the URL is your Steam ID3, and it should correspond to a folder in `~/.steam/root/userdata`.

ok question, if i wanted to write to the shortcuts.vdf file should i always use the "0" folder or always try to find the right id3 of the current user that is logged in? and what if there are multiple accounts on the system?

You should always write to the correct `shortcuts.vdf` file for the current user who is logged in. If there are multiple Steam accounts on the system, you need to find the correct `userdata` folder that corresponds to the user who is currently logged in and then write to the `shortcuts.vdf` file in that folder.

The code I provided earlier uses the UID of the currently logged in user to find the correct `userdata` folder, so it should work for multiple users on the same system. Here's a modified version of that code that writes "test" to the `shortcuts.vdf` file in the correct `userdata` folder:

```
# get the UID of the current user
CURRENT_UID=$(id -u)

# loop through all the userdata folders and find the one that belongs to the current user
for USERDATA_FOLDER in ~/.steam/root/userdata/*
do
    # get the UID of the user associated with the current userdata folder
    USER_UID=$(stat -c '%u' "$USERDATA_FOLDER")

    # if the UID of the current userdata folder matches the UID of the current user, then this is the right folder
    if [[ "$CURRENT_UID" -eq "$USER_UID" ]]
    then
        # extract the Steam ID3 from the userdata folder name
        USER_ID3=$(basename "$USERDATA_FOLDER")

        # output the Steam ID3
        echo "Steam ID3: $USER_ID3"

        # write "test" to the shortcuts.vdf file in the userdata folder
        echo '"Test Shortcut" { "AppName" "test" }' >> "$USERDATA_FOLDER/config/shortcuts.vdf"

        # output a message indicating that the data was written successfully
        echo "Data was written to shortcuts.vdf in $USERDATA_FOLDER"

        # break out of the loop, since we've found the right userdata folder
        break
    fi
done
```

This code uses the same method of finding the current user's `userdata` folder as before, but it now writes "test" to the `shortcuts.vdf` file in that folder. Note that this code only writes to the first `userdata` folder that corresponds to the currently logged in user, so it will not work if the user has multiple Steam accounts.
is there another way to find the current uid that doesnt end up going to the "0" folder?

Yes, you can use `ls` to list all the `userdata` folders and compare their ownership with the ownership of the `steam` binary file to find the correct folder. Here's how you can do it:

```
# get the UID of the steam binary file
STEAM_UID=$(stat -c '%u' $(which steam))

# loop through all the userdata folders and find the one that belongs to the current user
for USERDATA_FOLDER in ~/.steam/root/userdata/*
do
    # get the UID of the user associated with the current userdata folder
    USER_UID=$(stat -c '%u' "$USERDATA_FOLDER")

    # if the UID of the current userdata folder matches the UID of the steam binary file, then this is the right folder
    if [[ "$STEAM_UID" -eq "$USER_UID" ]]
    then
        # extract the Steam ID3 from the userdata folder name
        USER_ID3=$(basename "$USERDATA_FOLDER")

        # output the Steam ID3
        echo "Steam ID3: $USER_ID3"

        # write "test" to the shortcuts.vdf file in the userdata folder
        echo '"Test Shortcut" { "AppName" "test" }' >> "$USERDATA_FOLDER/config/shortcuts.vdf"

        # output a message indicating that the data was written successfully
        echo "Data was written to shortcuts.vdf in $USERDATA_FOLDER"

        # break out of the loop, since we've found the right userdata folder
        break
    fi
done
```

This code uses `stat -c '%u'` to get the UID of the steam binary file. It then compares the ownership of each `userdata` folder with the UID of the steam binary file to find the correct folder for the current user. Note that this code will only work if there is only one user logged into Steam at a time. If there are multiple users logged in at the same time, the ownership of the `steam` binary file may not match the ownership of the correct `userdata` folder.
